risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those eligible were listed in alphabetical order by surname. They were then selected randomly from the patient list, using a computer-generated random numbers table." "They were then randomly assigned to one of the two groups in this study: psychoeducation and mutual support group (n = 34) or standard care (n = 34)." Comment: The study explicitly describes use of a computer-generated random numbers table and random assignment, which indicates a proper random allocation sequence.

_evidence.method_quote: “Those eligible were listed in alphabetical order by surname. They were then selected randomly from the patient list, using a computer-generated random numbers table.” “They were then randomly assigned to one of the two groups in this study: psychoeducation and mutual support group (n = 34) or standard care (n = 34).” “the participants were then asked by the principal researcher to draw a sealed opaque envelope, in which a number card indicated to which group they had been allocated (1 = psycho-education group and 2 = routine care group).”
_evidence.method_summary: Eligible participants were randomly selected from an alphabetical list using a computer-generated random numbers table, then randomly assigned by sealed opaque envelope.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "the participants were then asked by the principal researcher to draw a sealed opaque envelope, in which a number card indicated to which group they had been allocated (1 = psycho-education group and 2 = routine care group)." Comment: This describes a sealed opaque envelope procedure, which is an allocation concealment mechanism intended to prevent foreknowledge of the upcoming assignment. Although sequential numbering is not explicitly stated, the article/extracted evidence identifies this as SNOSE with opaque and sealed features, and there is no signal that the recruiter could peek or predict assignments.

_evidence.concealment_quote: the participants were then asked by the principal researcher to draw a sealed opaque envelope, in which a number card indicated to which group they had been allocated (1 = psycho-education group and 2 = routine care group).

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Except for the principal researcher and the group instructor, all other clinic staffs were blinded to treatment allocation." Comment: This explicitly states that the principal researcher and the group instructor were not blinded, and it does not state that participants were blinded; in addition, the main outcomes were largely self-reported/subjective (e.g., FBIS, FAD, FSSI, SLOF), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence them. The objective hospital admission outcome does not remove the high risk for the study overall because the dominant outcomes were subjective.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “Except for the principal researcher and the group instructor, all other clinic staffs were blinded to treatment allocation. An independent assessor (a research assistant) was trained to undertake measurements at Time 1, 2 and 3 using a set of questionnaires.”

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "An independent assessor (a research assistant) was trained to undertake measurements at Time 1, 2 and 3 using a set of questionnaires." Comment: Outcome assessment was performed by an independent assessor rather than the treating team. The main outcomes were caregiver-reported questionnaires (FBIS, FAD, FSSI, SLOF) plus hospital admissions/length of rehospitalization from hospital/outpatient records. Although participants may have been unblinded, the paper explicitly states an independent assessor collected the questionnaire data and the record-based hospital outcomes are objective, so knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have biased outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: An independent assessor (a research assistant) was trained to undertake measurements at Time 1, 2 and 3 using a set of questionnaires.

outcomes.label: psychiatric hospital admissions / length of re-hospitalization

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Analysis of data was on an intention-to-treat basis. All subjects, who completed the intervention or not, were followed up over a 12-month post-intervention period." Comment: The paper reports per-arm post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcome (34 randomized and analysed in each arm in Table 3, with only 3 dropouts/absences in the psychoeducation group and 2 in routine care; total loss 7.8% and differential loss 2.9 percentage points). Reasons for dropout were reported and appear mainly logistical or participant-related rather than clearly outcome-driven, and the study states ITT analysis. This is consistent with low risk of attrition bias.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “pscho-education group (n = 3)”; “Reasons for dropout or discontinuation from the group interventions were mainly: insufficient time to attend (n = 2), patient’s mental state worsened (n = 3), not interested (n = 2), and the only person taking care of patient (n = 2).”

“routine care group (n = 2)”; “Reasons for dropout or discontinuation from the group interventions were mainly: insufficient time to attend (n = 2), patient’s mental state worsened (n = 3), not interested (n = 2), and the only person taking care of patient (n = 2).”

_evidence.notes: Flow information is reported for the caregiver sample; the paper states participants dropped out or were absent in more than four sessions and that all subjects were followed up and analysed on an intention-to-treat basis. The 34/34 analysed numbers are taken from Table 3, which presents outcome scores at Time 1, 2 & 3 for both groups.
